abstract: Fieldwork is vital for understanding political conflict, yet the Institutional Review Board (IRB) process often acts as a deterrent rather than a facilitator for such research. Current regulatory frameworks frequently fail to account for the unique uncertainties of international field research, particularly in volatile conflict zones. Drawing on personal experience navigating these hurdles while studying the Maoist conflict in India, this article identifies three primary obstacles: a federal regulatory system rooted in the biomedical sciences that is ill-suited for qualitative social science; a significant disconnect between "procedural ethics" (bureaucratic compliance) and "ethics in practice" (on-the-ground reality); and a persistent communication gap between researchers and review boards. To address these issues, I propose several reforms: bridging the divide between investigators and IRBs through continuous dialogue; streamlining approval timelines to reduce project delays; fostering better collaboration and protocol-sharing among scholars; and encouraging academic associations to take a more proactive role in advocating for policies that reflect the realities of field research.
